perturbation.original_text:
20. GRIEVANCE AND DISCIPLINARY PROCEDURE
20.1 If the Executive is dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision relating to him, including any decision to dismiss him, he will have the right to appeal to the Chairman of WPP plc, whose decision will be final.
20.2 If the Executive seeks to redress any grievance relating to his employment, the Executive should raise this in the first instance with the Chairman. If the matter is not satisfactorily resolved, the Executive should then apply in writing to the Board and the Board's decision will be final.
perturbation.changed_text:
20. GRIEVANCE AND DISCIPLINARY PROCEDURE
20.1 If the Executive is dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision relating to him, including any decision to dismiss him, the Executive may discuss the decision with the Chairman of WPP plc, whose decision will be final.
20.2 If the Executive seeks to redress any grievance relating to his employment, the Executive should raise this in the first instance with the Chairman. If the matter is not satisfactorily resolved, the Executive may then apply in writing to the Board and the Board's decision will be final.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "will have the right to appeal" to "may discuss", the contract removes the *guaranteed* right to appeal a disciplinary decision, potentially violating UK employment law regarding fair dismissal procedures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Employment Rights Act 1996 and ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
perturbation.law_citation: Employment Rights Act 1996, s. 94; ACAS Code of Practice
perturbation.law_explanation: The Employment Rights Act 1996, s. 94, establishes the right not to be unfairly dismissed, and the ACAS Code of Practice provides guidelines for fair disciplinary procedures, including the right to appeal. Changing "will have the right to appeal" to "may discuss" weakens this right, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.justification: The original contract guarantees the executive 'will have the right to appeal,' but the changed version softens this to 'may discuss.' This alteration, as detailed in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, undermines the executive's right to appeal disciplinary actions, potentially conflicting with the Employment Rights Act 1996, which aims to prevent unfair dismissals. The altered text removes a guaranteed right, and replaces it with the possibility of a discussion, which is a significant weakening of the employee's position. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Employment Rights Act 1996. This supports the argument that the change introduces a legal contradiction by potentially violating the employee's right to a fair disciplinary procedure including the right to appeal.

perturbation.original_text:
6. REMUNERATION
6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.changed_text:
6. REMUNERATION
6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 per annum. The salary will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the "fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum" creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance if these benefits are legally required or understood as part of the total remuneration package. If these benefits are not provided elsewhere in the contract or in practice, it introduces legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Employment Rights Act 1996, Section 8, requires employers to provide a written statement of employment particulars, including details of remuneration and benefits. Omitting the fixed benefits allowance from the contract could be construed as a failure to provide complete information about the employee's remuneration package, violating this section of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies both a salary and a fixed benefits allowance, whereas the changed contract removes the fixed benefits allowance. The Law Explanation states that the Employment Rights Act 1996, Section 8 requires employers to provide a written statement of employment particulars, including details of remuneration and benefits. By omitting the fixed benefits allowance, the changed text fails to provide complete information about the employee's remuneration package, potentially violating the Employment Rights Act 1996, as the modified text removes details of benefits, and the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' states that a legal contradiction happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text:
10. SICKNESS ABSENCE
10.1 If the Executive cannot attend work due to sickness or injury, the Executive will keep the Chairman informed of his condition and, where the absence lasts for a period of seven calendar days or more, the Executive will (at the request of the Company) produce a doctor's certificate to the Company in respect of his absence. (a) Provided the Executive complies with the Company's sickness absence notification and certification requirements, the Executive shall be entitled to receive his full salary and contractual benefits during any period of sickness absence not exceeding 26 weeks in any rolling period of 12 months. These payments shall be inclusive of any Statutory Sick Pay due. No payment of salary will be made during any subsequent period of absence when the Executive is eligible to receive benefits under the Group income protection plan referred to in sub clause 9.2.
perturbation.changed_text:
10. SICKNESS ABSENCE
10.1 If the Executive cannot attend work due to sickness or injury, the Executive will keep the Chairman informed of his condition and, where the absence lasts for a period of seven calendar days or more, the Executive will (at the request of the Company) produce a doctor's certificate to the Company in respect of his absence. (a) Provided the Executive complies with the Company's sickness absence notification and certification requirements, the Executive may receive his full salary and contractual benefits during any period of sickness absence. These payments shall be inclusive of any Statutory Sick Pay due. No payment of salary will be made during any subsequent period of absence when the Executive is eligible to receive benefits under the Group income protection plan referred to in sub clause 9.2.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall be entitled to receive his full salary and contractual benefits during any period of sickness absence not exceeding 26 weeks" to "may receive his full salary and contractual benefits during any period of sickness absence", the contract removes the *guaranteed* right to receive full salary during sickness absence, making this discretionary and violating employee sick pay rights according to UK law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) under the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 and related regulations.
perturbation.law_citation: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992, s. 151
perturbation.law_explanation: The Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 and related regulations mandate that eligible employees are entitled to Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) during periods of sickness absence. The altered clause makes the provision of full salary discretionary, which is less than the legal entitlement to SSP, putting the contract in conflict with UK law.

It’s paid by your employer for up to 28 weeks. This guide is also available in Welsh (Cymraeg) . You cannot get less than the statutory amount. You can get more if your company has a sick pay scheme (or ‘occupational scheme’) - check your employment contract. There are different sick pay rules for agricultural workers . perturbation.justification: The original contract states the executive 'shall be entitled' to receive full salary during sick leave, but the changed text modifies this to 'may receive'. This introduces discretion where there was previously an obligation. According to the GOV.UK snippet, 'You cannot get less than the statutory amount' for Statutory Sick Pay (SSP), and the original text guarantees full pay (inclusive of SSP) for a period. The changed text, by making full salary discretionary, could result in the executive receiving less than their statutory entitlement, thus creating a potential contradiction with employment law regarding sick pay.
